title: Chained plying on the wheel
subtitle: Making a three-ply yarn from a single bobbin using the Navajo chain loop method
excerpt: Chained plying, also called Navajo plying, produces a three-ply yarn from a single bobbin of singles without needing a separate lazy kate or three bobbins of the same weight. The method passes the singles through a chain of loops, each loop becoming one section of the three-ply. It is particularly useful for preserving colour sequences in hand-dyed singles.
sourceNotes: Chained plying is a traditional technique with roots in Navajo weaving preparation. Modern wheel-spinning use of the method is widely documented in spinning guild manuals. Method described here is observation-led practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: In chained plying, the loop formed by reaching through an existing loop and pulling a fresh bight of singles yarn through it. Each chain loop becomes one ply section in the three-ply yarn.

A single strand of spun yarn that has not yet been plied with another strand. Singles are typically over-twisted (more twist than needed for balance) to offset the reverse twist applied during plying.

content.text: structure: you reach through a loop and pull a new loop of

through it, like a crochet chain. Treadling the wheel in S-twist (reverse of the original Z-twist singles) plies all three strands at once. The resulting yarn is balanced, approximately three times the diameter of the original singles, and retains colour sections intact.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair, loose clothing, and cord away from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair before starting.

items.name: Spinning wheel (set to S-twist / reverse treadle direction)

content.text: Step 1: Set up the first loop

Tie the singles onto the leader of the empty bobbin. Pull about 60 cm of singles from the bobbin. Fold the yarn back on itself to form a large loop and hold the loop with your non-dominant hand. Now reach through the loop and pull a fresh bight (loop) of yarn through it. You now hold two loops and the parent singles in three strands between your hands.

Begin treadling in S-twist (reverse of spinning). Hold the three strands in your upstream hand and allow twist to run into them. With your downstream hand, control the rate the three strands feed onto the bobbin. Maintain even tension across all three strands: if one strand sags, the ply will be uneven.
Step 3: Form each new chain loop

As you approach the end of the current loop section (about 10-15 cm before the loop closes), reach through the remaining loop and pull a fresh bight through it. This forms the next chain loop. Keep the join smooth: if the loop closes too early, the join will be a thick spot. The chain loop lengths can be any length from 5 cm to 50 cm; consistent lengths within a skein are easier to manage.
Step 4: Wind off and set the twist

When the source bobbin is empty, wind the three-ply off the wheel bobbin onto the niddy-noddy to form a skein. Tie the skein in four places with loose figure-8 ties. Soak the skein in lukewarm water for 20 minutes, squeeze out the water, snap the skein firmly between both hands several times to set the twist, and hang to dry under no tension.

items.problem: The yarn is under-plied and still has residual twist when relaxed
items.cause: Treadling too slow relative to the singles' original twist level
items.fix: The singles need more S-twist per length. Increase treadling speed or slow the take-up.

items.problem: The join between chain loops is consistently thick
items.cause: The loop closed before the new loop was pulled through, creating a doubled section
items.fix: Pull the new chain loop through when there is still at least 15 cm remaining in the current loop.

items.problem: One of the three strands sags and produces an inconsistent ply
items.cause: Uneven tension across the three strands in the downstream hand
items.fix: Hold all three strands between index finger and thumb, using slight independent finger pressure to equalise tension.
